subject: Physics
solution:
Energy of photon $=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{12500}{980}=12.75 \mathrm{eV}$

Energy of electron in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit is given by

$\mathrm{En}=\frac{-13.6}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}}-\mathrm{E}_{1}=-13.6\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \frac{-1}{1^{2}}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 12.75=13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}} \frac{-1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4$

$\therefore$ Electron will excite to $\mathrm{n}=4$ We know that ' $R^{\prime} \propto n^{2}$

$\therefore$ Radius of atom will be $16 \mathrm{a}_{0}$

subject: Physics
solution:
In Bohr's atom, the energy levels are $E_n = -\frac{E_0}{n^2}$ where $E_0$ is the ground state energy.
Given $E = -0.04E_0$, we have $-\frac{E_0}{n^2} = -0.04E_0$, which gives $n^2 = 25$, so $n = 5$.
The angular momentum in Bohr's model is $L = n\hbar = n\frac{h}{2\pi}$.
Therefore, $\frac{2\pi L}{h} = \frac{2\pi}{h} \times n\frac{h}{2\pi} = n = 5$.

subject: Physics
solution:
The angular momentum of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by Bohr's quantization rule:

$L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

Given that the angular momentum is $\dfrac{3h}{\pi}$, we can equate the two expressions:

$\dfrac{nh}{2\pi} = \dfrac{3h}{\pi}$

Solving for $n$, we get:

$n = 6$

The energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by the formula:

$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$

Substituting $n = 6$ into the energy formula:

$E_6 = -\dfrac{13.6}{6^2} \text{ eV}$

$E_6 = -\dfrac{13.6}{36} \text{ eV}$

$E_6 \approx -0.377 \text{ eV} \approx -0.38 \text{ eV}$

Answer: $-0.38$

solution:

Thus, R∝A13

Volume of nucleus is V=43πR3⇒V∝R3⇒V∝A.

Now, the density of nucleus is given by ρ=MV=mA43πR03A=m43πR03

Thus, density is independent of A.

Hence, only A and E are correct.

solution:
According to the question,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{n} \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{\mathrm{mv}} \\
& \text { or } \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \text { or } \mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\
& \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qv}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \text { or, } \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r} \cdot \mathrm{r}} \\
& \text { or, } \mathrm{r}^2=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{qB}} \\
& \text { or, } \mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{qB}}} \\
& \text { i.e., } \mathrm{r} \propto \mathrm{n}^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

solution:
Smallest wavelength of lyman
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R\left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2}\right)$
$R = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{91} \text{ nm}^{-1}$
$\lambda_{max}$ for balmer series
$n_1 = 2 \rightarrow n_2 = 3$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_B} = R\left(\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_B} = \dfrac{1}{91}\left(\dfrac{5}{36}\right)$
$\lambda_B = \left(\dfrac{91 \times 36}{5}\right) = 655.2 \text{ nm}$
$\lambda_{max}$ paschen
$n_1 = 3 \rightarrow n_2 = 4$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_p} = \dfrac{1}{91}\left(\dfrac{1}{3^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2}\right) = \dfrac{1}{91} \times \dfrac{7}{144}$
$\lambda_p = \left(\dfrac{91 \times 144}{7}\right) = 1872 \text{ nm}$
$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_P - \lambda_B = 1872 - 655.2$
$\Delta\lambda = 1216.8$
$\Delta\lambda \approx 1217$

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius r of any nth Bohr's orbit is given by

r=r0n2Z   ...1

where, r0 is Bohr's radius, n is the principal quantum number and Z is the atomic number.

Thus, for Helium ion,

r1=r0222= 2r0   ...2

And, for the Beryllium ion,

r2=r0424=4r0   ...3

Divide equation (3) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

r2r1=4r02r0=2

Hence, x=2.

subject: Physics
solution:
The momentum of a photon is given by $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$.

From Rydberg's formula, the wave number is $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$.

For the first line of the Balmer series, the transition is from $n_2 = 3$ to $n_1 = 2$.

$p_1 = \dfrac{h}{\lambda_1} = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} \right) = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9} \right) = \dfrac{5hR}{36}$

For the second line of the Balmer series, the transition is from $n_2 = 4$ to $n_1 = 2$.

$p_2 = \dfrac{h}{\lambda_2} = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right) = hR \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right) = \dfrac{3hR}{16}$

The ratio of the momenta is:

$\dfrac{p_1}{p_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{5hR}{36}}{\dfrac{3hR}{16}} = \dfrac{5}{36} \times \dfrac{16}{3} = \dfrac{20}{27}$

Comparing with $\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$, the possible values are $\alpha = 20$ and $\beta = 27$.

Answer: $20$ and $27$

subject: Physics
options: In the ground state of Rutherford's model electrons are in stable equilibrium. While in Thomson's model electrons always experience a net-force.
An atom has a nearly continuous mass distribution in a Rutherford's model but has a highly non-uniform mass distribution in Thomson's model
A classical atom based on Rutherford's model is doomed to collapse.
The positively charged part of the atom possesses most of the mass in Rutherford's model but not in Thomson's model.
solution:
According to Rutherford's model, most of the mass of the atom is concentrated at the centre (nucleus) and the electron revolves around it.

As the electron is moving in a circular motion, it is accelerated. According to Maxwell accelerated charged particle loses its energy in the form of electro-magnetic radiation.

Therefore, a classical atom based on Rutherford's model is doomed to collapse.
